description: Recorded BRIDGE agent run (2026-07-18 21:40) used for deterministic demo replay. Live mode via backend/server.py reproduces this flow with real Claude tool-use calls.

events.text: Maria Alvarez, 67F. HFrEF (EF 30%), CAD s/p STEMI with LAD stent. Discharged 5 days ago after HF exacerbation. Spironolactone increased at discharge.
NP panel view seeded: 6 monitored patients with agent-assigned risk tiers (LOW/MED/HIGH).
Home monitoring, day 5: resting HR 92 (baseline 68), weight +2.7 kg / 5 days, SpO2 94% (baseline 97), BP 128/80.
Single-lead ECG: NEW peaked T waves vs normal baseline morphology.
MONITORING ALERT — multi-signal deviation from 90-day baseline. Agent activated.

HR +6 SD with 3-day uptrend, >2 kg weight gain, falling SpO2 — pattern consistent with recurrent volume overload. New peaked T waves 5 days after a spironolactone increase on top of sacubitril/valsartan and CKD 3a raises concern for hyperkalemia. Interviewing patient; screening red flags first.
Hi Maria, it's your care team's monitoring assistant. Your home readings changed today and I'd like to check on you. First — any chest pain, pressure, or fainting?
No chest pain, no. I just feel more short of breath than usual today.
Thank you. Is it harder to breathe when you lie flat — have you needed extra pillows or a recliner at night?
Yes — the last two nights I slept sitting up in the recliner. Lying flat makes it hard to breathe.
That's helpful. Are you taking all your medicines, including the water pill dose that was changed at the hospital?
Yes, everything — including the new dose. My legs look puffier than last week too.

DIFFERENTIAL — 1) Recurrent ADHF / volume overload (weight +2.7 kg, orthopnea, edema, HR uptrend, SpO2 drop). 2) Hyperkalemia (peaked T waves; spironolactone just increased + ARNI + CKD 3a). 3) Ischemia — less likely (no chest pain, no ST change). Triage: URGENT — needs labs within 12–24h; no red flags requiring ER now.

URGENT page — Dana Kim, NP (covering)
S: PT-1042 Alvarez, 67F HFrEF EF 30%, day 5 post-ADHF discharge.
B: Spironolactone increased 12.5→25 mg at discharge; on sacubitril/valsartan; CKD 3a (Cr 1.4).
A: Resting HR 92 vs baseline 68 (+6 SD, 3-day uptrend), weight +2.7 kg/5d, SpO2 94%, NEW peaked T waves on single-lead ECG. Reports orthopnea and worsening dyspnea; denies chest pain/syncope. Concern: recurrent HF exacerbation + possible hyperkalemia.
R: Recommend BMP + BNP before Monday visit; consider holding spironolactone pending K. Clinic slot booked Mon 8:40 AM.
Agree with your read. Co-signed the BMP + BNP for tomorrow AM draw at the lab near her. Hold spironolactone until K back. See her Monday 8:40. Good catch.

Maria, I shared your readings with Dana Kim, NP. Plan: a blood test tomorrow morning (order is in), pause the spironolactone (water pill) until she reviews it, and clinic Monday 8:40 AM. Go to the ER or call 911 right away if you have chest pain, fainting, palpitations, or you can't catch your breath at rest.
Monday clinic: K+ 5.8, BNP rising. Spironolactone held, furosemide increased 40→80 mg, repeat labs Wednesday. Readmission averted — caught on a Saturday night, treated in clinic on Monday.
